statement.title: Statement
statement.content:
Description
You are given two integer arrays $ a $ and $ b $ , both of length $ n $ , and a total number of turns $ k $ .

Alice and Bob play a game by taking turns modifying array $ a $ . Alice goes first. The game lasts for exactly $ k $ turns.

On their turn, a player must choose an index $ i $ ( $ 1 \leq i \leq n $ ) and perform one of the following operations:

- Add: Increase $ a_i $ by $ b_i $ , i.e., set $ a_i := a_i + b_i $ .
- Subtract: Decrease $ a_i $ by $ b_i $ , i.e., set $ a_i := a_i - b_i $ .

After the $ k $ -th turn is complete, the final score is calculated as the maximum non-empty subarray sum of the modified array $ a $ . Alice's goal is to maximize the final score, while Bob's goal is to minimize the final score.

Assuming both players play optimally to achieve their respective goals, determine the final score.

The maximum non-empty subarray sum of $ a $ is defined as $ \max_{1 \leq i \leq j \leq n} S(i, j) $ , where $ S(i, j) = a_i + a_{i+1} + \cdots + a_j $ . Note that empty subarrays are not considered.

Input Format
Each test contains multiple test cases. The first line contains the number of test cases $ t $ ( $ 1 \le t \le 10^4 $ ). The description of the test cases follows.

The first line of each test case contains two integers $ n $ and $ k $ ( $ 1 \le n \le 2\cdot 10^5 $ , $ 1 \le k \le 2\cdot 10^5 $ ) — the length of the arrays and the total number of turns.

The second line contains $ n $ integers $ a_1,a_2,\ldots,a_{n} $ ( $ -10^9 \le a_i \le 10^9 $ ) — the elements of array $ a $ .

The third line contains $ n $ integers $ b_1,b_2,\ldots,b_{n} $ ( $ 0 \le b_i \le 10^9 $ ) — the elements of array $ b $ .

It is guaranteed that the sum of $ n $ over all test cases does not exceed $ 2\cdot 10^5 $ .

Output Format
For each test case, print a single integer — the final score after $ k $ turns, assuming both players play optimally.

Examples
Sample 1
Input
5
5 200000
3 -1 9 -5 4
0 0 0 0 0
4 5
10 10 10 10
1 1 1 1
3 1
2 -7 3
1 11 3
3 2
2 -7 3
1 11 3
1 1
-3
2
Output
11
41
9
3
-1

Note
For the first test case:

- Any move can't change the array $ a $ because all elements in $ b $ are zero.
- The maximum non-empty subarray sum of this final array is $ 3 + (-1) + 9 = 11 $ .

For the second test case, one of the possible optimal sequences of moves is:

- Alice adds $ b_3=1 $ to $ a_3 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [10, 10, 11, 10] $ .
- Bob subtracts $ b_1=1 $ from $ a_1 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [9, 10, 11, 10] $ .
- Alice adds $ b_3=1 $ to $ a_3 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [9, 10, 12, 10] $ .
- Bob subtracts $ b_1=1 $ from $ a_1 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [8, 10, 12, 10] $ .
- Alice adds $ b_4=1 $ to $ a_4 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [8, 10, 12, 11] $ .
- The maximum non-empty subarray sum of this final array is $ 8+10+12+11 = 41 $ .

For the third test case, one of the possible optimal sequences of moves is:

- Alice adds $ b_2=11 $ to $ a_2 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [2, 4, 3] $ .
- The maximum non-empty subarray sum of this final array is $ 2 + 4 + 3 = 9 $ .

For the fourth test case, one of the possible optimal sequences of moves is:

- Alice adds $ b_2=11 $ to $ a_2 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [2, 4, 3] $ .
- Bob subtracts $ b_2=11 $ from $ a_2 $ . Array $ a $ becomes $ [2, -7, 3] $ .
- The maximum non-empty subarray sum of this final array is $ 3 $ .

hints.title: Hint 1
hints.content: If Alice makes a move and Bob immediately performs the opposite move on the same index (and vice versa), what happens to the array?

hints.title: Hint 2
hints.content: How does the parity of $$$k$$$ affect who makes the last move and therefore who can enforce the final uncancelled change?

hints.title: Hint 3
hints.content: Using the cancellation idea, can the entire game be reduced to a simpler one with exactly $$$k \bmod 2$$$ effective moves?

hints.title: Hint 4
hints.content:
If Alice changes $$$a_i$$$, only subarrays containing $$$i$$$ can improve. What are the maximum-sum subarrays that
end at $$$i$$$
and
start at $$$i$$$
, and how can combining them give the best subarray passing through $$$i$$$?

solutions.title: Tutorial
solutions.content:
2158C - Annoying Game
Let $$$S(a)$$$ denote the maximum non-empty subarray sum of array $$$a$$$. We determine the final score by analyzing the game based on the parity of $$$k$$$, assuming optimal play from both Alice and Bob.
First, consider the case where $$$k$$$ is
even
. Bob has a strategy to ensure the final score is at most the initial $$$S(a)$$$. Since Bob plays the even-numbered turns, he can adopt a cancellation strategy. Immediately after Alice modifies an index $$$i$$$ by adding $$$b_i$$$, Bob can modify the same index $$$i$$$ by subtracting $$$b_i$$$. This ensures that after every pair of turns, the array is restored to the state it was in before Alice's turn. After $$$k$$$ turns, the array is identical to the initial array. Thus, Bob forces the result $$$\le S(a_{\text{initial}})$$$.
We must verify if Bob can force the score lower, or if Alice can guarantee at least $$$S(a_{\text{initial}})$$$. Alice adopts the following strategy: Let the subarray $$$[l, r]$$$ be the subarray yielding the initial maximum sum. Alice identifies an index $$$p$$$ such that $$$l \le p \le r$$$ and $$$b_p$$$ is the maximum value among all $$$b$$$ in that range. In her first turn, Alice adds $$$b_p$$$ to $$$a_p$$$. For all subsequent turns, Alice adopts the cancellation strategy against Bob: if Bob modifies index $$$x$$$, Alice immediately modifies index $$$x$$$ in her next turn to restore it.
This strategy ensures that just before Bob's very last move (turn $$$k$$$), the array has exactly one modification: $$$a_p$$$ is increased by $$$b_p$$$. Bob must now make a move to minimize the score. To reduce the sum of the subarray $$$[l, r]$$$, Bob must choose an index $$$q$$$ within the range $$$[l, r]$$$ and subtract $$$b_q$$$. The new sum becomes $$$S(a_{\text{initial}}) + b_p - b_q$$$. Since Alice chose $$$p$$$ such that $$$b_p$$$ is maximal in that range, $$$b_p \ge b_q$$$. Consequently, $$$S(a_{\text{initial}}) + b_p - b_q \ge S(a_{\text{initial}})$$$. Bob is forced to choose $$$q=p$$$ to simply undo Alice's first move; otherwise, the score remains strictly greater than $$$S(a_{\text{initial}})$$$. Thus, for even $$$k$$$, the final score is exactly $$$S(a_{\text{initial}})$$$.
Next, consider the case where $$$k$$$ is
odd
. Alice has a strategy to maximize the result of a single independent move. She performs her desired modification on turn 1. For any subsequent move by Bob, Alice uses her next turn to cancel it. Since Alice makes the last move (turn $$$k$$$), she ensures all of Bob's actions are neutralized. Conversely, Bob can prevent Alice from achieving anything more than a single move by canceling Alice's actions during the first $$$k-1$$$ turns. The game reduces to Alice making exactly one move $$$a_i \leftarrow a_i + b_i$$$ to maximize $$$S(a)$$$.
To solve this efficiently, we use prefix and suffix maximums. Let $$$L[i]$$$ be the maximum sum of a non-empty subarray ending at $$$i$$$, and $$$R[i]$$$ be the maximum sum of a non-empty subarray starting at $$$i$$$. If Alice modifies index $$$i$$$, the maximum sum passing through $$$i$$$ is the sum of the maximum prefix ending at $$$i$$$ and the maximum suffix starting at $$$i$$$, adjusted for modified value. Since both $$$L[i]$$$ and $$$R[i]$$$ include the original $$$a_i$$$, we subtract $$$a_i$$$ once to avoid double counting, then add $$$b_i$$$ (denoting Alice modification). The final answer is the maximum of the initial score and the best possible modified sum:
$$$$$$ \max_{1 \le i \le n} (L[i] + R[i] - a_i + b_i) $$$$$$
